summary: GitHub Copilot, powered by GPT-3, will accelerate code production but also accelerate bug propagation, since AI-generated code trained on open source repositories will likely have even higher defect rates than human-written code. The author warns that when programmers become accustomed to auto-completed code that compiles, they may fail to deeply review it, creating dangerous scenarios especially as AI systems are increasingly built on AI-generated scaffolding.
key_points: Copilot represents impressive engineering but will produce code with more than 50 bugs per 1000 lines based on its training data
The industry average defect rate is 15-50 bugs per 1000 lines of code, and open source projects often have even more
AI-generated code that compiles easily may reduce human scrutiny, compounding the bug problem
The recursive danger of using AI to build scaffolding for AI systems creates new vectors for bug propagation
GPT-3 doesn't understand meaning, making it fundamentally unsuited to the meaning-laden work of software construction
money_quotes: With Copilot, bugs will be transmitted faster than the speed of thought.
Building software is building a small world. It's about meaning, and we know GPT-3 doesn't understand meaning.
Now imagine your scaffolding itself is written mostly by Copilot. Bugs will propagate in new ways, via systems that build systems.
When programmers get used to auto-complete code that compiles, how deep will they go into it? Will they review it carefully?
key_insight: AI-powered code generation tools like Copilot will accelerate not just coding speed but bug propagation, especially as developers grow complacent reviewing auto-generated code that superficially appears correct.
